steps: Ensure the wheelchair is stable and the user is not seated in it.
Check if the tire is punctured or just deflated; inflate to the pressure printed on the tire sidewall.
If punctured, remove the wheel using the quick-release axle or wrench.
Replace the inner tube or use a patch kit on the puncture.
Refit the wheel and confirm it spins freely and brakes grip properly.
Stop using the wheelchair immediately; failing brakes are a safety risk.
Do not attempt to ride the wheelchair until it has been serviced.
Stop using the wheelchair immediately; a cracked frame can collapse.
Do not attempt a DIY weld or brace.
Check whether the tear affects the seat's support; small surface tears are cosmetic.
Clean the area around the tear with mild soap and let it dry.
Apply a vinyl/fabric repair patch over the tear, pressing firmly.
If the tear is larger than a hand's width or the seat sags, request a seat replacement.
Unplug the bed from mains power.
Do not open the motor housing; it must be serviced by a technician.
Check the remote's cable is firmly plugged into the control box under the bed.
Replace the remote's batteries if it is a wireless model.
Unplug the bed from mains power for 60 seconds, then plug back in to reset the control box.
Test each button; if only some buttons fail, the remote itself needs replacement.
Stop adjusting the bed; a bent frame can jam the motor or collapse.
Keep the bed in a flat, stable position until it is serviced.
Remove bedding and inspect the size of the tear.
For small tears, apply a waterproof mattress repair patch.
Fit a waterproof mattress protector to prevent moisture getting into the foam.
If foam is exposed or the tear is longer than a hand's width, request a mattress replacement.
